terms.definition: A large town with many buildings, roads, shops, hospitals, and schools where lots of people live and work.
terms.exampleUsage: Mumbai is the biggest city in India.

terms.definition: A home inside a tall building, also called an apartment. Many families live in different flats in the same building.
terms.exampleUsage: Nandita's aunt lives in a flat on the fifth floor.

terms.definition: An old-style building in Mumbai where many families live in small rooms and share common spaces like corridors and bathrooms.
terms.exampleUsage: In the chawl, all the children played together in the common area.

terms.definition: A train that runs short distances within a city, stopping at many stations. Mumbai's local trains are the lifeline of the city.
terms.exampleUsage: Millions of people travel by local train in Mumbai every day.

terms.definition: The movement of vehicles on a road. When too many vehicles are on the road, there is a traffic jam.
terms.exampleUsage: The traffic in Mumbai can be very heavy during office hours.

terms.definition: An area in a city where people live in small, crowded homes made of tin sheets or other materials, often without proper water or toilets.
terms.exampleUsage: Many families in the slum share a common water tap.

terms.definition: A person in Mumbai who picks up lunch boxes from homes and delivers them to offices, and returns the empty boxes.
terms.exampleUsage: The dabbawala delivered Amma's lunch to Papa's office on time.

terms.definition: When harmful things like smoke, waste, and chemicals make the air, water, or land dirty and unhealthy.
terms.exampleUsage: Air pollution from vehicles makes it hard to breathe in big cities.

terms.definition: A fast electric train system in cities that often runs underground or on elevated tracks to avoid road traffic.
terms.exampleUsage: The Delhi Metro helps people travel quickly across the city.

terms.definition: A paved path on the side of the road meant for people to walk on safely, away from vehicles.
terms.exampleUsage: We should always walk on the footpath, not on the road.
